transcript: Music Along the coast of Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, humpback whales are on the move. Music Every year, these magnificent animals travel from Alaska in search of warmer waters. The Hawaiians-needed sanctuary to this endangered species, a unique setting where ocean and lava collide. Hawaii is the only place in the U.S. where humpbacks breed and nurse their young. with very little fat, calves would soon freeze to death in the cold waters of Alaska. Mothers come to these clear, shallow waters to avoid predators. If there's a catch, there's no food here. The moms go hungry, losing up to 40% of their weight raising their calves. It's a sacrifice that pays off. Humpback populations are increasing. The park is just one place to see these giants of the deep. But they are not its biggest attraction. Two massive volcanoes are. The first is Kilauea, one of the most active volcanoes on Earth. The lava lake inside this crater can be more than 750 feet deep. Its lava flows can reach the sea 10 miles away. Kilauea is so big that even its vents and craters are huge. Starting in 1983, one of these vents erupted for 35 years. It produced so much lava that it covered the coast road, burying it more than 100 feet deep. To the west is the second volcano, and it's the biggest on the planet. This is Mauna Loa, rising more than 56,000 feet above its base, much taller than even Mount Everest. The park exists thanks to eruptions over hundreds of thousands of years, where hardened lava forms a solid foundation for life. Close to the source, poisonous gas fumes escape from hot vents, called fumaroles. This landscape is often swept clean by molten lava. It's one of the most barren and toxic environments on Earth. Nature has had to adapt to living in this land of fire. A wingless lava cricket. After an eruption, she is the first creature to take up residence in this hostile landscape. The ultimate pioneer. No larger than a fingernail, she survives by eating morsels of food that blow in with the ocean breeze. Nobody knows where she comes from, where she goes, or how long she lives. In many ways, she's a mystery. At some point known only to soil, only hardened lava, where the Ohia Lehua tree takes root. It is one of the first plants to do so after an eruption Growing from tiny windblown seeds this incredibly rugged tree anchors its roots in cracks and crevices when toxic volcanic gases blow its way, the plant closes its breathing pores, called stomata, in essence, holding its breath. The Hawaiians revere these trees. When they speak of people who are skilled, strong, and beloved, they refer to them as pu'u lehua, or lehua blossoms. They grow up to 100 feet tall and can live over a thousand years. Over time, the tough volcanic specialists will grow from gangly individuals into incredible tropical forests. Eighty percent of Hawaii's native trees are ohia lehua. Another reason for their success is their aerial root systems, which draw in moisture from the Pacific's tropical air. With roots in the air and digging deep into the lava below, the ohia tree ensures its survival and provides a home for other creatures the forests are a biological treasure a sanctuary for rare and fantastical native species some are endemic like the happy face spider found only in Hawaii unlike many spiders she doesn't wait to catch her prey in a web but instead actively hunts for food when she senses an insect nearby she throws a sticky silk over it just like a lasso and hauls it in. There are also a few unwelcome visitors, like the Jackson's three-horned chameleon. This species was brought to Hawaii from East Africa in the 1970s. it is considered an invasive species hunting native snails insects and spiders it poses a threat to the delicate balance of this magical forest. But there's another native creature here that fights to keep the balance. This caterpillar is a special resident in Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, commonly known as an inchworm. These little herbivores have evolved to blend in amongst the ohia blossoms. But the cute little inchworm has a killer cousin. Which has a dramatically different approach to getting food. It's not pollen or nectar he's after. these are some of the only carnivorous caterpillars on the planet. He latches onto the branch with his rear prolegs and waits for a victim to come close. With six front tarsal claws ready to snatch his prey, he sits perfectly still until something triggers his rear sensory bristles. Faster than the blink of an eye, he eats his prey alive. A magical forest growing on lava that's home to a carnivorous caterpillar might sound like fiction, but this park is where legends, lore, and real life combine. Trees, rocks, plants and animals can all have connections to deities and spirits. Nowhere is that connection stronger than within the heart of Kīlauea in the Halema'uma'u crater. Tradition holds this is the home of the goddess Pele. According to legend, Pele is the daughter of the Earth Mother Papa and Sky Father Wākia, two of the most ancient and revered of all Hawaiian gods. Pele is known as she who shapes the sacred lands. Ancient legends inspire people today and shape much of life throughout Hawaii. over 1,000 years Hawaiians have cherished this land they continue to strive to find balance to protect their home including Hawaii volcanoes National Park dr. Sam Ohuong is an advocate for integration of Hawaiian values and knowledge. Our traditions place great value on nature. The park is a protected place but that protection extends beyond the park to all of the islands of Hawaiiʻi. It's a sacred part of who we are as a people. Hawaiians call their ancestor gods Auma Kua. And they can take the form of native plants and animals. Such a form as a lau, a physical manifestation of a deity. When the gods are also your family, and the elements of nature their physical presence, your relationship with nature is fundamentally transformed. Of all the Hawaiian traditional values, the one most globally recognized today is aloha. Empathetic compassion. Love. The aloha that you extend to family extends further in Hawaiian thought to all of the elements of nature around you and your aina. The lands of your place. This emerges as aloh Aloha Aina A deep appreciation and love for all the features of the land Such that you realize that you are not whole without your place And that the fate and health of your place is your own fate, your own health Hawaiians believe that this applies across all lands, on every island, and around the world. That connection extends to all of the creatures on Earth, no matter where they call home or how far they roam. A green sea turtle called Honu by Hawaiians is making her way to the park. The Honu is an omakua, a friendly spirit that watches over and protects Hawaiian families. After 20 years at sea, she's heading for the shallow and inviting waters along Hawaii Volcanoes National Park. She's been lucky so far. Only one in a thousand baby turtles survive to adulthood. But her greatest fight may lay ahead. A tiger shark off the coast of Hawaii Volcanoes National Park is looking for a hefty meal. A green sea turtle is just the ticket. Out swimming the shark isn't an option. But a clever defense might save her life. Turning on her side makes her too big a meal for the shark to get its jaws around. She's not tired, but the shark is using up a lot of energy. The reins went out over Ronwn and the turtle continues on her journey. Green sea turtles have been protected throughout the United States since 1978. But the park was founded long before then. It was established in 1916 by President Woodrow Wilson in order to protect these wild and wonderful landscapes. Hawaii Volcanoes National Park is one of the oldest national parks in the U.S., but Hawaiians were here first. The park is filled with a rich cultural tradition. it's thought that early hawaiians carved these petroglyphs more than 500 years ago using stones to etch the images deep into the cooled lava. The carvings in the park form the largest concentration of petroglyphs anywhere in Hawaii. On land, people, plants and animals learn to live alongside lava. But it also shapes life in the ocean. During an eruption, flowing lava creates channels underground. These channels are lava tubes, created when molten lava cools on the surface but continues to flow underneath. Underneath Along the edges of the park where lava meets the sea These lava tubes offer a different type of opportunity for some of the oceans's fiercest sea-dwellers. Here, in the confines of the tubes, white-tipped reef sharks find safety from larger predators, like the tiger shark. They look like they're asleep, but are simply in a deep state of calm, something that's easier to achieve in the safety of the lava tubes. Amidst the labyrinth exists another species that's found a clever way to survive in this fish-eat-fish world, the Hawaiian cleaner wrasse. He provides an invaluable service that the other reef dwellers take full advantage of. Like his name suggests, he cleans all of the residents, big and little, relieving them of unwanted parasites, broken scales, or mucus. The fish crowd around him, jostling to be next his striking colors and the way he moves is a signal to other fish that he is here to help. But can every fish be trusted? The Hawaiian cleaner wrasse now undertakes a somewhat terrifying task. the scariest of ocean dwellers rely on him to help them survive. What the reef shark might gain as a meal is nothing compared to what it gets by staying clean of parasites. life here is connected, creating unique opportunities in the heart of the most fiery wonders within the park. When 2,000 degree lava meets the sea, things get explosive. The heat from the lava raises the water temperature. Minerals disperse, a vital food source for the ocean's smallest organisms. Plankton. These tiny creatures are the precursors to much of the wildlife that we know in the sea. Plankton includes a wide range of organisms, such as bacteria, algae, and microscopic animals. In one teaspoon of seawater, as many as a million single-celled plankton can be found. Without them, entire marine ecosystems could collapse. And along the coast of the Big Island of Hawaii, the abundance of plankton attracts a creature that seems out of this world. For divers with a desire for a unique underwater experience,'s a once-in-a-lifetime encounter to be had. The Hahalua is also known as the Reef Manta Ray. Despite a formidable wingspan of up to 14 feet, they surprisingly only eat tiny plankton. These gentle giants pose no threat to people. mannose have the largest brain size of any fish they are highly intelligent and recent experiments show self-aware this trait is also shared by dolphins, primates, and elephants, so it puts the manta in a pretty select club. Inside the park, Pele wakes up. When her mind is set to destroy, there's little that can escape her wrath. But once in a while, pay attention. But once in a while, Pele's mercy can create a sanctuary that is unlike any other place on Earth. This is a Kapuka forest. Woodland spared by lava flows. Now extremely isolated. Its seclusion creates the opportunity for unimaginable islands of habitat. This forest has been cut off by continual lava flows going back at least 8,600 years. has evolved independently from other areas on the island. Thanks to a process known as adaptive radiation, the isolation of these capucas is behind the incredible number of unique species that live here. From as few as 250 species, species that live here. From as few as 250 original insect species, Hawaii is now home to more than 7,500. Species may be found here that exist nowhere else on earth, not even in forests of few Thank you for watching. Species may be found here that exist nowhere else on Earth, not even in forests a few miles away. But there is a looming threat. Rapid ohia death is a foreign fungal disease that the native trees are defenseless against. We'll be right back. is a foreign fungal disease that the native trees are defenseless against. Even with the protection of the park, up to 90 one of the most spectacular examples of evolution on the planet. Meet the Scarlet Hawaiian Honeycreeper. There's been at least 59 different species of honeycreepers throughout Hawaiian history. Some scientists suggest they all descended from a single ancestor. The flowers of the ohia tree are their main source of food. curvy beak is a special adaptation that allows better access to nectar deep within. What may have started out as one species of bird evolved into dozens of different types each in their own little world where once there were 59 species only 17 exist today the rest are sadly extinct and there's another bird found here who's custom-built for this place. This is a cousin of the scarlet honeycreeper. He doesn't feed on nectar. He eats insects. An incredibly rare bird. There are approximately 1,000 left in the wild. The Hawaiian woodpecker. He locates his prey with his lower beak, using it like a pickaxe to dig. The curved top beak gives him reach life in the least expected places. Thank you for watching. The Ohia lehua tree has another surprise in store. Not only do its roots in the sky help to capture precious moisture. They also delve deep beneath the ground too, emerging into an alien world. below the surface of Hawaii volcanoes National Park there is a hidden and mysterious world like no other deep inside the lava tubes that crisscross underground the roots dangle through the cave ceiling they help to transport and provide an essential ingredient for life water while the lava tubes may seem like they'd be barren of life. It turns out these roots provide an environment for rare insects. Cave biologists from around the U.S. have been studying the communities of animals living in the lava tubes. Recently, a research team discovered a remarkable number of new species. The new discoveries hint at what may yet be found we're still learning how these hidden places are connected and essential to life above ground where some lava tubes are seemingly inaccessible, others welcome us into their mysterious depths. This tunnel was created over 500 years ago by a river of molten lava that exceeded temperatures of 2,000 degrees Fahrenheit. is nahurston Lava Tube, one of the most visited sites in Hawaii Volcanoes National Park. It's part of the network of tunnels that spans more than 40 miles from Kīlauea. Descending 3,600 feet beneath the surface, it is the longest and deepest lava tube network on the planet. The park has more than half a million visitors every year, but humans are not the only species to keep coming back. After thousands of miles and more than 20 years at sea, a female green sea turtle returns to the park's inshore waters. This is her homecoming debut. But first, she needs a bit of a cleanup. Over the years, her shell has become covered in algae it doesn't hurt her but it does slow her down up until now there wasn't anything she could do about it. Today, she's in luck. Yellow and black tangs offer a unique cleaning service. It's a symbiotic relationship. The turtle gets clean, and the fish get a meal. Before long, she'll find her way back to the beach where she was born. There, she'll lay her eggs and continue the circle of life. As some residents return, others leave. Winter break is over. It's time for the humpbacks to begin their migration back to the cooler waters of Alaska. Their numbers have grown. their time in Hawaii has been productive and the calves are strong enough to embark on the 3,000 mile journey the humpbacks keep in constant contact through complex vocalizations Thanks for watching! on the 3,000-mile journey. The humpbacks keep in constant contact through complex vocalizations. Some of their low frequency calls can travel through water up to 10,000 miles. Next year, the whales will return to this special place, like so many visitors to the park choose to do. The park was born from lava, and it's how it continues to grow. Eruptions of Kilauea and Mauna Loa produce new layers of land that make the island bigger. But the park is so much more than just volcanoes and lava. Every day in Hawaii Volcanoes National Park can reveal something new and unexpected. The land and its inhabitants live in a precious balance. Hawaiians knew it. and we recognize it today. By understanding how important this place is to all of us, we become part of something bigger than just the park. Something essential, memorable, legendary.
